symbols: pipe organ — sacred music and Cecilia's patronage of musicians, representing the harmony of earthly praise directed toward heaven
crown of flowers — virginal purity and the crown of martyrdom, the garland of virtue that adorns the faithful soul
raised hand — ecstatic prayer and communion with the divine, the soul reaching toward God beyond the visible world
upward gaze — spiritual rapture and Cecilia's legendary ability to hear heavenly music inaudible to others
richly embroidered garments — noble birth and the dignity of holiness, the soul adorned with grace
description: A three-quarter-length portrait of a young woman against a dark background. She wears a sumptuous black velvet dress with an ornate golden-embroidered brocade cape or stole draped over one shoulder, lined in crimson red. A jeweled brooch with a red gemstone adorns her neckline. Her light brown hair flows freely, crowned with a wreath of pink and white flowers and a golden halo. She gazes upward and to the right with an expression of spiritual rapture, her left hand raised with open palm. Her right hand rests on a set of metal pipe organ tubes at the lower left of the composition.
theological_description: Saint Cecilia, venerated since the early centuries of Christianity, is the patron saint of musicians and sacred music. According to her hagiographic tradition, she was a Roman noblewoman who consecrated her virginity to God, and on her wedding day, while musical instruments played, she 'sang in her heart to the Lord alone.' This interior music became the basis for her association with sacred music. She was martyred for her faith, reportedly surviving an attempt at suffocation in her own bath and three strikes of an executioner's sword, living for three more days during which she distributed her possessions to the poor and arranged for her house to become a church. Gentileschi portrays her in the moment of spiritual ecstasy, her gaze lifted beyond the visible world to hear the celestial harmonies. The organ beside her is not merely an attribute but a theological statement: true worship flows from the interior song of the soul to God. While Cecilia is not a biblical figure, the theology of sacred music she embodies is deeply scriptural, rooted in the Psalms' exhortation to praise God with instruments and in Colossians 3:16's call to sing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs.

scripture_refs.reason: The great doxology commanding praise with every instrument — the scriptural foundation for Cecilia's patronage of sacred music
Singing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs with gratitude in your hearts to God — the Pauline theology of interior worship Cecilia embodies
Making melody to the Lord in your heart — directly echoing the tradition that Cecilia sang in her heart to God alone
The sound of harps before the throne and the new song of the redeemed — the heavenly music Cecilia's upward gaze perceives
Sing to Him a new song, play skillfully with a shout of joy — the vocation of sacred musicianship
